original_hypothesis.title: Permissionless GaugeFactory.createGauge enables attacker-controlled gauges that may be whitelisted downstream, enabling reward/fee capture or DoS
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: GaugeFactory.createGauge is permissionless and deploys a new GaugeV2 with attacker-chosen constructor parameters (reward token, LP token, distribution, bribe addresses, genesis manager). If any downstream system (e.g., GaugeManager/Voter/UI) treats GaugeFactory output as implicitly trusted, an attacker can create deceptive gauges for legitimate LP tokens, route users to stake, and then disrupt operations (e.g., set DISTRIBUTION to attacker EOA so notifyRewardAmount never happens, or set bribe addresses to attacker-controlled contracts). Even without direct theft, this can cause operational confusion and loss of rewards/fees for stakers who deposit into the wrong gauge.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Permissionless gauge creation is a frequent real-world ops issue when downstream relies on 'factory provenance' instead of explicit whitelisting. Phase A notes createGauge is permissionless regardless of roles.
deployment_assumptions: Downstream components (GaugeManager/Voter/registry/UI) accept or display any GaugeFactory-created gauge without an allowlist
Users can be induced to stake into the attacker-created gauge via on-chain discoverability (not requiring phishing; e.g., auto-indexers)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No in-scope GaugeManager/Voter code provided; must verify whether gauges are explicitly registered/whitelisted elsewhere

proof_of_signal.pattern: function createGauge(...) external returns (address) { last_gauge = address(new GaugeV2(...)); __gauges.push(last_gauge); } // no access control

preconditions: Attacker can pay deployment gas for GaugeV2 creation
At least one downstream path treats GaugeFactory gauges as valid without separate admin registration
Users or automation can interact with the attacker gauge for staking

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Factory provenance may be misinterpreted as authorization; GaugeV2 parameters are entirely attacker-controlled at deploy time
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None to create; downstream trust decision determines impact

operational_questions: How are gauges registered in GaugeManager/Voter—do they require explicit admin action?
Do UIs/indexers filter gauges by GaugeManager mappings or simply enumerate GaugeFactory.__gauges?
Can a malicious gauge be paired with a legitimate LP token and attract deposits?
validation_requirements: Trace gauge discovery path in production UI and subgraphs
Check whether GaugeManager uses GaugeFactory.length()/gauges(i) as source of truth
Simulate creating a gauge for an existing LP and observe whether it appears as stake target

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy a GaugeV2 via GaugeFactory for a popular LP TOKEN with attacker-chosen DISTRIBUTION/internal_bribe
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If discovered/whitelisted automatically, allow deposits; collect any fees/rewards routed to attacker-controlled bribe/rewarder
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Optionally trigger operational DoS by configuring bribe/rewarder to revert on notify calls (if any path uses it)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users stake into non-canonical gauges; rewards/fee distribution breaks; protocol reputation damage
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium (requires UI/registry fixes and user migration)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: New GaugeV2 deployments from GaugeFactory where TOKEN matches an existing canonical pair LP
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Duplicate gauges for same LP; deposits into non-registered gauges
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Gauge constructor args with unusual DISTRIBUTION or bribe addresses

validation_result.mechanism: missing access control on gauge creation
validation_result.impact_summary: GaugeFactory.createGauge lacks any access control, allowing anyone to deploy GaugeV2 with arbitrary parameters, pollute the __gauges registry, and manipulate last_gauge—potentially causing downstream confusion or front-running if GaugeManager reads last_gauge after its own createGauge call.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The GaugeFactory.createGauge function at GaugeFactory.sol lines 35-39 has no access control modifier (no onlyOwner, onlyAllowed, or any msg.sender check). Any external caller can deploy a new GaugeV2 with fully attacker-controlled constructor parameters (_rewardToken, _ve, _token, _distribution, _internal_bribe, _external_bribe, _isPair, _genesisManager). The newly created gauge is appended to __gauges[] and stored as last_gauge. This is a concrete missing check / access control defect.

Two immediate on-chain impacts are verifiable from in-scope code alone:
1. __gauges array pollution: The __gauges array grows with unauthorized entries, and the length()/gauges(i) public accessors expose these to any downstream consumer.
2. last_gauge race condition: If any out-of-scope contract (like GaugeManager/Voter) calls createGauge and then reads last_gauge in a subsequent transaction, an attacker can front-run with their own createGauge call, causing last_gauge to point to the attacker's gauge instead of the legitimate one.

The GaugeV2 is deployed with GaugeFactory as its Ownable owner (since msg.sender in the GaugeV2 constructor context is the GaugeFactory). This means the attacker doesn't become owner of the gauge directly, but they fully control its constructor parameters including DISTRIBUTION (who can notify rewards), internal_bribe (where fees are forwarded), and genesisManager.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: GaugeFactory.sol lines 35-39 clearly show createGauge is external with zero access control. Comparing with other state-changing functions in the same contract: setRegistry (line 28) checks owner(), activateEmergencyMode checks EmergencyCouncil modifier, setDistribution/setGaugeRewarder/setInternalBribe check onlyAllowed modifier. createGauge is the only state-modifying function without any access control.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control modifier or msg.sender check. Any address can call this function, deploying a GaugeV2 with arbitrary constructor parameters and modifying shared state (last_gauge and __gauges[]).

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls GaugeFactory.createGauge with arbitrary parameters
2. new GaugeV2(...) is deployed with attacker-chosen _distribution, _internal_bribe, _external_bribe, _genesisManager, _token (LP)
3. last_gauge is set to the new gauge address (shared mutable state)
4. __gauges.push(last_gauge) appends to the factory's gauge registry
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entire function body executes without any authorization check. Contrast with setRegistry at line 28 which requires owner() == msg.sender, or setDistribution at line 66 which requires onlyAllowed modifier. createGauge at line 35 has neither.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Downstream components (GaugeManager/Voter/registry/UI) accept or display any GaugeFactory-created gauge without an allowlist
Users can be induced to stake into the attacker-created gauge via on-chain discoverability
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show GaugeFactory deployed via upgrades.deployProxy with initialize([]) (V1/upgradeable/deployBribeFactoryV2Upgradeable.js). No GaugeManager or Voter deployment scripts are available in scope. BlackholePairAPIV2 deployment (deploy-pair-api.js) shows it uses gaugeManager for gauge lookups, not GaugeFactory directly. However, the in-scope BlackholePairAPIV2._pairAddressToInfo uses gaugeManager.gauges(_pair) not GaugeFactory.__gauges, suggesting downstream may use a separate allowlist. The last_gauge race condition remains a risk if GaugeManager reads it after calling createGauge.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
The code defect (missing access control) is concretely verifiable. Attack scenarios:

1. Registry pollution: Attacker calls createGauge repeatedly, inflating __gauges[] array. Any off-chain indexer or UI enumerating GaugeFactory.length()/gauges(i) would see attacker gauges alongside legitimate ones.

2. last_gauge front-running: If the out-of-scope GaugeManager calls GaugeFactory.createGauge() then reads last_gauge in a subsequent step, an attacker can front-run between these operations to substitute their malicious gauge.

3. Deceptive gauge for legitimate LP: Attacker creates a gauge for a popular LP token with DISTRIBUTION set to attacker EOA (so no rewards ever flow) and internal_bribe set to attacker contract (capturing fee claims). Users who discover and stake in this gauge lose rewards.

Scenarios 1 and 2 are code-level defects independent of configuration. Scenario 3's impact depends on downstream trust decisions.
validation_result.impact_assessment:
The missing access control is a definite code defect. Direct impacts:
- __gauges array can be polluted by anyone (storage griefing)
- last_gauge can be overwritten by anyone (potential race condition with GaugeManager)
- Attacker-parameterized GaugeV2 contracts are registered in the factory's official list

Conditional impacts (depending on downstream):
- Users could stake LP tokens in non-canonical gauges and lose rewards
- Fee distribution could be misdirected to attacker-controlled bribe contracts
- Operational confusion requiring manual cleanup
recommended_mitigations: Add access control to createGauge: require(owner() == msg.sender || IPermissionsRegistry(permissionsRegistry).hasRole('GAUGE_ADMIN', msg.sender), 'NA');
Alternatively, restrict createGauge to only a specific caller (e.g., the GaugeManager/Voter contract) by storing its address and checking msg.sender
Avoid using last_gauge as shared state; instead return the address directly and have the caller store it
Add a mapping to validate gauge authenticity: mapping(address => bool) public isGauge, set in createGauge, and check in admin functions
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy GaugeFactory via proxy with initialize(). 2. From a non-owner EOA, call createGauge with attacker-controlled parameters. 3. Verify the call succeeds and __gauges contains the new gauge. 4. Verify last_gauge points to attacker's gauge. 5. Simulate a front-running scenario where attacker's createGauge overwrites last_gauge before a legitimate consumer reads it.
uncertainties: GaugeManager/Voter contracts are not in scope, so we cannot verify whether they use __gauges or last_gauge as source of truth for gauge registration
We cannot verify whether any on-chain indexer, subgraph, or UI enumerates GaugeFactory.__gauges directly
The race condition on last_gauge is only exploitable if some contract reads it after another contract's createGauge call, which we cannot verify without GaugeManager code
additional_testing_needed: Review GaugeManager/Voter source code to determine if they read last_gauge or enumerate __gauges
Check subgraph/indexer queries to see if they use GaugeFactory events or __gauges enumeration
Test front-running scenario with actual GaugeManager deployment to validate last_gauge race condition
